warning: AI-assisted monitoring. Verify against official legislation before product approval, export, label declaration, or customer compliance confirmation.

rules.sourceTitle: Standard for Food Additive Use - GB2760-2015
rules.notes: This standard specifies the varieties, scope of use, and maximum usage levels of food additives in China. Specific limits must be verified against the full text of GB 2760-2015.
rules.evidenceQuote: Standard for Food Additive Use - GB2760-2015

rules.notes: Automated extraction failed: {"error":{"code":503,"message":"This model is currently experiencing high demand. Spikes in demand are usually temporary. Please try again later.","status":"UNAVAILABLE"}}
Verification of approved food additives and their conditions of use is required via the EU Additives Database and Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.
rules.evidenceQuote: This database can serve as a tool to inform about the food additives approved for use in food in the EU and their conditions of use. It is based on the Union list of food additives. This list is in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

rules.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, toxins, and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Verification of food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins, and metabolites is required as laid down in Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.
rules.evidenceQuote: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 on microbiological criteria for foods, applicable from 1 January 2006, lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites, such as Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins and histamine in specific foods.

rules.notes: This technical regulation covers general labelling requirements for pre-packaged food, which would include allergen declaration, applicable to seasoning and snack products.

rules.parameter: Permitted food additives and their maximum limits

rules.parameter: General labelling and display requirements for pre-packaged food

rules.limit: Verification required for specific production, import, registration, and circulation conditions

rules.limit: Verification required for specific food categorization

rules.limit: Verification required for specific regulations pertaining to additives, microbiology, contaminants, salt/sodium, allergens, and labeling

Manufacturers and importers must verify compliance with these regulations for all relevant aspects, including additive limits, microbiology, contaminants, salt/sodium, allergens, and labeling for seasoning and snack products.
rules.evidenceQuote: Pangan Olahan 281 Peraturan

rules.parameter: Ingredients of food or additives, and methods of production, processing, use, cooking, or preservation
rules.limit: Must conform to standards and criteria established by the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare. Verification of MHLW's standards and criteria is required.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Sanitation Act (Act No. 233 of 1947, Last Version: Act No. 36 of 2023)
rules.notes: These are general standards and criteria for food and additives.
rules.evidenceQuote: the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare may establish the criteria for the methods... or may establish standards for the ingredients... food or additives that do not conform to such standards must not be produced... or sold.

rules.limit: Only additives (excluding natural flavoring agents and generally consumed substances used as additives) specified by MHLW as unlikely to cause harm are permitted. Verification of MHLW's positive list is required.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Sanitation Act (Act No. 233 of 1947, Last Version: Act No. 36 of 2023)
rules.notes: This establishes a positive list system for food additives.
rules.evidenceQuote: Additives... must not be sold... except for cases that the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare specifies as unlikely to cause harm to human health

rules.parameter: Residues of agricultural chemicals, feed additives, and pharmaceuticals for animals
rules.limit: Must not exceed quantities specified by the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare as unlikely to cause harm. Verification of MHLW's specified limits/standards is required.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Sanitation Act (Act No. 233 of 1947, Last Version: Act No. 36 of 2023)
rules.notes: This refers to the Positive List System for Agricultural Chemical Residues in Food.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food in which substances that are the ingredients of agricultural chemicals... feed additives... and pharmaceutical products... remain in a quantity exceeding the quantity that the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare specifies as unlikely to cause harm to human health... must not be produced, imported... or sold

rules.limit: Prohibited, unless specified by MHLW as unlikely to cause harm to human health
rules.sourceTitle: Food Sanitation Act (Act No. 233 of 1947, Last Version: Act No. 36 of 2023)
rules.notes: This applies to all food and additives meant for sale.
rules.evidenceQuote: those which contain or bear toxic or harmful substances or are suspected to contain or bear such substances; provided, however, that this does not apply when the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare specifies that the substances are unlikely to cause harm to human health

rules.parameter: Labelling and advertising of food or additives
rules.limit: Must conform to standards and criteria established by the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare. Verification of MHLW's standards and criteria is required.
rules.sourceTitle: Food Sanitation Act (Act No. 233 of 1947, Last Version: Act No. 36 of 2023)
rules.notes: This covers general labelling and advertising requirements.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare may establish standards for labeling food or additives... food or additives... must not be labeled or advertised in a manner that does not conform to such standards or criteria.

rules.limit: Prohibited if contaminated with pathogens or suspected to be so, and likely to cause harm to human health
rules.sourceTitle: Food Sanitation Act (Act No. 233 of 1947, Last Version: Act No. 36 of 2023)
rules.notes: This applies to all food and additives meant for sale.
rules.evidenceQuote: those which are contaminated with pathogens or are suspected to be so, and which are likely to cause harm to human health

rules.limit: Verification is required as limits vary by additive and food type; specific regulations must be consulted.
rules.evidenceQuote: Different limits apply, depending on the additive and the type of food products. Use the Guidance Information on Requirements for Use of Food Additives to learn more about the different types of additives and the corresponding regulations that impose the limits.

rules.notes: Requires consulting the official legislation for detailed limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: The food safety regulatory limits can be found in SFA's Legislation.

rules.limit: Must be assessed for safety by SFA
rules.notes: Mandatory pre-market assessment for use in food sold in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.

rules.notes: Prohibition of non-permitted additives.
rules.evidenceQuote: Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.

rules.limit: Must have technological justification and not present health risks to consumers
rules.notes: Criteria for allowing food additives.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives are allowed when: there is a technological justification they do not present health risks to consumers

rules.limit: All food businesses must comply with regulatory limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.
If your food product contains an additive that is not permitted, do not import or sell it.

rules.limit: Verification is required using the List of Permitted Food Additives or Food Additives Search tool.
rules.evidenceQuote: Download the List of Permitted Food Additives or use the Food Additives Search tool to check if your additives are allowed.

rules.parameter: Use of two or more Class II chemical preservatives (e.g., sorbic acid, benzoic acid)
rules.limit: Use the Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives to determine permitted levels.
rules.evidenceQuote: If two or more Class II chemical preservatives are to be used in the food (e.g. sorbic acid or benzoic acid), use the Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives to calculate the permitted levels that should be used.

rules.limit: Food businesses must be aware of common contaminants and conduct laboratory tests as needed.
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: Food businesses must be aware of the contaminants that are commonly found in their food products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.

rules.limit: Must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.

rules.parameter: Food products containing contaminants exceeding regulatory limits
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food or food products contain contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed the regulatory limits, do not import or sell them.

rules.limit: All food businesses must comply with regulatory limits.
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents i.e. contaminants in food.

rules.parameter: Microbiological standards, heavy metals, mycotoxins, marine biotoxins, veterinary drug residues, pesticide residues, other incidental constituents
rules.limit: Verification is required by consulting relevant SFA documents/lists.
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: For more details, please refer to the relevant documents below: Microbiological standards Ready-to-eat food Non-ready-to-eat food (till 3 Mar 2024) Non-ready-to-eat food (from 4 Mar 2024) Heavy metals Heavy metals Mycotoxins and Marine biotoxins Mycotoxins and Marine biotoxins Veterinary drug residues Veterinary drug residues Pesticide residues Singapore's regulatory limits Codex MRLs (if there are no regulatory limits specified in the Food Regulations) Other incidental constituents Other incidental constituents

rules.foodCategory: Various food commodities (seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.limit: Compliance with SFA's established Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) is required. Verification is required.

rules.notes: MRLs can be downloaded from the SFA website.
rules.evidenceQuote: SFA has also assessed the risk of the presence of other veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, and has established a list of maximum residue limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues for compliance by food businesses with effect from 1 May 2020.

rules.foodCategory: Meat and food derived from meat
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Applies to meat and any food derived from meat.
rules.evidenceQuote: Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of the following compounds: diethylstibestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-3-hexene) hexoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-n-hexane) dienoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-2, 4-hexadiene)
Your food or food product must also comply with the labelling requirements.

rules.foodCategory: Prepacked foods (seasoning powder, seasoning sauce, snack seasoning, ready-to-eat snacks)
rules.limit: Must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels.
rules.notes: Applies to prepacked foods for sale in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: All ingredients and additives used in food products must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels.

rules.limit: Must comply with microbiological standards for ready-to-eat food. Verification is required.

rules.evidenceQuote: Non-ready-to-eat food (till 3 Mar 2024) Non-ready-to-eat food (from 4 Mar 2024)

rules.limit: Not required to be tested for TPC.

rules.evidenceQuote: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food are not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).

rules.notes: These 10 allergens require mandatory labeling if present as an ingredient or food additive.

rules.evidenceQuote: sulphite as the residue sulphur dioxide for 10 or more milligrams per kilogram of food

rules.evidenceQuote: If there is a risk of a food product being affected by allergen cross-contamination... the following statement of precautionary allergen labeling is suggested: “ The plant, equipment or production line used to produce the product is also used to process○○” or the synonymous terms.

rules.notes: Specific limits and detailed regulations for various food safety aspects are available in the national regulatory databases.
rules.evidenceQuote: 衛生福利法規檢索系統

rules.notes: Regulations exist for the use of food additives.
rules.evidenceQuote: วัตถุเจือปนอาหาร

rules.notes: Regulations exist for the use of probiotic microorganisms in food.
rules.evidenceQuote: การใช้จุลินทรีย์โพรไบโอติกในอาหาร

rules.notes: Food additives and color additives must be listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173, 73, 74) or be Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS) or prior-sanctioned. Verification against the inventory is required.
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives and color additives that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 172, 173 and Parts 73, 74 respectively), and flavoring substances evaluated by FEMA* and JECFA*. Generally Recognized as Safe ("GRAS") substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Parts 182 and 184). Substances approved for specific uses in foods prior to September 6, 1958, known as prior-sanctioned substances (21 CFR Part 181).

rules.notes: Prohibited substances are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189). Delisted color additives are in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30). Verification against the inventory is required.
rules.evidenceQuote: Prohibited substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) as prohibited from use in food (labeled as "PROHIBITED" or "PROHIBITED WITH EXCEPTIONS"). Delisted color additives in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30) (labeled as "DELISTED").

rules.notes: The 9 major food allergens recognized by the U.S. are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
rules.evidenceQuote: The major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame. ...declaring sesame as the 9th major food allergen recognized by the United States. The change was effective on January 1, 2023.

rules.notes: Guidance level for 3-MCPD in acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces.
rules.evidenceQuote: 3-Monochloropropane-1,2-Diol (3-MCPD) Acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces Guidance Level 1 ppm CPG 500.500

rules.notes: Action level for total aflatoxins in general foods.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Foods Action Level 20 ppb CPG 555.400

rules.notes: Action level for total aflatoxins in peanuts and peanut products.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Peanuts and peanut products Action Level 20 ppb CPG 570.375

rules.notes: Contaminant levels (tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, etc.) for poisonous or deleterious substances in human food are published in guidance documents and regulations (21 CFR parts 109 and 509) and consolidated in the Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool. Verification is required.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA has published a variety of contaminant levels for poisonous or deleterious substances in human food in various guidance documents and regulations for industry. For ease of reference, the Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list of these levels. Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) parts 109 and 509.

rules.notes: Advisory statements (e.g., 'may contain [allergen]') are not required by law and should only be used to address unavoidable cross-contact after good manufacturing practices have been incorporated. They must be truthful and not misleading.
rules.evidenceQuote: Consumers may also see advisory statements such as “may contain [allergen] or “produced in a facility that also uses [allergen].” Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes in their facility and have taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact... advisory statements should not be used as a substitute for adhering to current good manufacturing practices and must be truthful and not misleading.

rules.notes: The CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact and undeclared allergens. Facilities must put written procedures in place.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA’s “Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food” rule (CGMP & PC rule, 21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements applicable to establishments that manufacture, process, pack, or hold human food. The CGMP & PC rule includes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens.

rules.notes: Cochineal extract and carmine must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.
rules.evidenceQuote: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine... must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

rules.notes: All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 must identify it on their labels.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels so consumers who are sensitive to the dye can avoid it.

rules.notes: The FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling. Compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the “gluten-free” claim were issued on August 12, 2020.
rules.evidenceQuote: On August 2, 2013, the FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling... On August 12, 2020, the FDA issued a final rule to establish compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods... bearing the “gluten-free” claim.

rules.notes: Food labels must identify the food source of all major food allergens. This can be done in parentheses following the ingredient name (e.g., 'lecithin (soy)') or in a 'contains' statement immediately after or next to the ingredient list (e.g., 'Contains wheat, milk, and soy.').
rules.evidenceQuote: The law requires that food labels identify the food source of all major food allergens used to make the food... The name of the food source of a major food allergen must appear: In parentheses following the name of the ingredient... — OR — Immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a “contains” statement.

rules.parameter: Specific Allergen Declaration (Tree Nuts, Fish, Crustacean Shellfish)
rules.notes: The specific type of tree nut (e.g., almonds, pecans, walnuts), and the species of fish (e.g., bass, flounder, cod) and Crustacean shellfish (e.g., crab, lobster, shrimp) must be declared.
rules.evidenceQuote: The law also requires that the type of tree nut (for example, almonds, pecans, walnuts), and the species of fish (for example, bass, flounder, cod) and Crustacean shellfish (for example, crab, lobster, shrimp) to be declared.

rules.notes: Various sulfiting agents must be declared on food labels when present in food at a concentration of ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
rules.evidenceQuote: Various sulfiting agents, including sodium bisulfite, are allowed as food ingredients. But due in part to adverse reactions to them... they must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
